title: Archangel Gabriel (Monthly) — St. Bessarion of Egypt & St. Hilarion the Younger
narrative:
title": "Archangel Gabriel (Monthly) — St. Bessarion of Egypt & St. Hilarion the Younger

narrative": "title\": \"Archangel Gabriel (Monthly) — St. Bessarion of Egypt & St. Hilarion the Younger

narrative\": \"The Monthly Commemoration of the Holy Archangel Gabriel the Annunciator, St. Bessarion the Great of Egypt the Desert Father, and St. Hilarion the Younger the Monk-Martyr

On the sixth of Sene, the holy Church celebrates the monthly commemoration of the holy Archangel Gabriel, the great angel of annunciation whose name means 'Man of God' or 'Strength of God' and who stands in the tradition as the messenger par excellence of the divine economy: it is Gabriel who appears to Daniel in the visions of the latter days, Gabriel who stands before Zechariah in the Temple and announces the birth of John the Forerunner, Gabriel who is sent to the Virgin Mary in Nazareth with the word that changes the history of the world. The monthly commemoration of Gabriel in the Ethiopian calendar reflects the Tewahedo tradition's particularly rich angelology: the angels are not merely literary devices or symbolic figures but personal beings who stand before the throne of God, who carry the divine word from heaven to earth, and whose ministry to the human race is as active and as constant as the intercession of the saints.

The theology of Gabriel in the Ethiopian tradition centres on his role as the angel of the Incarnation: it is his voice that makes the Incarnation begin, his word 'Hail, full of grace' that initiates the divine condescension into human nature, and the tradition has therefore associated him with every subsequent moment in which God's presence breaks into human history. The monthly commemoration of Gabriel is the calendar's acknowledgment that the annunciation does not cease: God is still sending his messenger to the human heart, still uttering the word that calls forth a new creation, still waiting for the human 'Yes' that makes the Word flesh in the world. Gabriel is the angel of every vocation — not only Mary's — and the community that gathers monthly around his feast is a community that presents itself anew each month as the recipient of the divine word and the potential vessel of the divine indwelling.

Saint Bessarion the Great of Egypt is one of the giants of the desert tradition, a contemporary of the great Antony and Macarius whose sayings are preserved in the Apophthegmata Patrum alongside those of the greatest names of Egyptian monasticism. Bessarion was known above all for the radicalism of his poverty and his mobility: unlike many of the desert fathers who established fixed cells and received disciples there, Bessarion wandered, carrying nothing, sleeping nowhere in particular, dependent entirely on what God and the hospitality of others provided. One of the most celebrated of his sayings records that when a disciple asked him how to achieve salvation, Bessarion remained silent for a long time and then said only: 'Be like the dead.' The saying is the tradition's most compressed theology of the monastic life: the monk who is dead to the world cannot be hurt by it, cannot be distracted by it, cannot be mastered by it, and in this freedom from the world's power he is more fully alive to God than any living arrangement of the ordinary world can make possible.

Saint Hilarion the Younger is a monk-martyr of a later period whose witness bridges the monastic and martyrological streams of the tradition: a man who had embraced the ascetic life and who then found himself in the path of persecution and had to choose between survival and faithfulness, between the monk's ordinary dying to the world and the martyr's extraordinary dying in the world. Hilarion's death for the faith is the tradition's statement that the monastic life is not a shelter from the demands of witness but a preparation for them: the monk who has spent years dying to himself in the cell has already rehearsed the death that martyrdom requires, and when the moment of external persecution comes, the internal discipline has already created in him the capacity to endure it. Bessarion and Hilarion together on this sixth day of Sene present two modes of the single monastic witness: the wandering father who was dead to the world in life, and the monk-martyr who was dead to the world in death. Amen.
